name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
This game is shockingly better than half of all 40k games and probably has a fraction of the budget behind it. The controls are stupidly simple, and it comes with a buttload of campaigns. All its downsides seem to stem from it being abandoned. Minor bugs exist but can be circumvented with a quick save and load. Vehicle combat could be better, especially with the troop transport mechanic, and it only features a small portion of races and factions in the 40k universe for lore purposes. 

This was a gem, and if they made an old world version I feel like I would have enjoyed it just as much.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
This is a really great turn-based strategy game that has everything we could want: great artillery, powerful titans, a huge selection of tanks and some very effective shock troops that we will need to capture objectives
We of course also have all the insanity and fanaticism in our superiors we will ever need to defend the Imperium

We can right-click on all units and read a lot about them which makes choosing between the many variants a lot easier
If you feel like it you can savescum in this game to your heart's content

There are several campaigns and the ones where we get to use titans are imo the best

My one complaint is that we as usual only get to play the Imperium and Orks and I guess I'll never get to control the legendary Eldar titans. A minor grudge and I had fun playing this

Very recommended!

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
I was on the fence with this game for a long time. The main thing holding me back was the price. Holy pricey for a game with low production value batman! I read many reviews here and other places, browsed the Slitherine forums for ahwile, and decided to bite and fork over 50 hard earned dollars. Of course 4 days later it goes on sale 50% off! Needless to say, "steam" is coming out of my ears right now...but I digress.

The game is fantastic. Not quite as deep as "Panzer Corps", but deep enough to make you think about what you're doing. Do I pick up another support unit for my core force or another armor division? Do I deploy in one giant wedge formation or split my force into two battlegroups? Do I upgrade my artillery units to Deathstrike missiles for maximum effect against armor or do I keep my Earthshakers for better versatility? Do I use a turn to rest and refit my elite units or do I press on? Every mission had me asking myself these questions...the first time I played them of course.

The game has ZERO replay value; once you know how the scenarios will unfold almost all these questions will be answered for you. For this reason alone it's very hard for me to recommend the game at full price. If you're a big fan of 40k and wargames I can say this is a winner, but it's way, way, WAY overpriced. Wait for a sale, or get more value for your dollar and pick up another wargame.

I will close by saying that after 100+ hours of play I can say that most (but certainly not all) of the negative reviews I have read are from people who thought this was 40k tabletop on PC and are disappointed to find out that it's not, or are from people who are not wargamers and somehow ended up buying this. It's a solid wargame with no replay value and a way too hefty pricetag. If $50 is chump-change to you, then I recommend this to any wargamer.

One thing i forgot to mention...the "manual" is garbage. How can you not explain, or for that matter even mention "Zones of Control". This is an absolutely critical mechanic to understand if you want to succceed and enjoy a game like this on all but the easiest difficulty. If you're not well versed in wargames and are thinking of picking this up I strongly suggest you look up ZOC on forums or whatever. You will get alot more out of the game, trust me.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
A Thrilling Tactical Masterpiece in the Warhammer 40K Universe

Warhammer 40K: Armageddon is an incredibly satisfying and strategically rich turn-based strategy game that immerses players in the dark and gritty world of the 41st millennium. Set during the epic conflict on the war-torn world of Armageddon, the game offers both long-time fans and newcomers to the Warhammer universe a chance to command vast armies in thrilling, high-stakes battles.

Immersive Warhammer 40K Setting

What truly shines in Armageddon is its deep-rooted connection to the iconic Warhammer 40K lore. The game does an excellent job of bringing the grimdark atmosphere to life, with its grim narration and dramatic setting. From the towering Imperial Guard tanks to the savage orc hordes, every faction feels authentic, and every engagement carries the weight of a desperate war for survival.

Strategic Depth

The core of the game’s appeal is its turn-based tactical combat. The battlefield is a chessboard of opportunity, where every decision counts. Whether you’re leading an armored column of Leman Russ tanks or orchestrating a pincer movement with elite infantry squads, the game’s mechanics encourage thoughtful, calculated strategies. The challenge of balancing resource management, unit placement, and timing of attacks creates a rewarding sense of accomplishment.

Diverse Factions and Units

The game offers a variety of factions to command, each with its unique units, strengths, and playstyles. The Imperial Guard's resilience and firepower contrast sharply with the Orks' chaotic and unpredictable nature, while the Tyranids bring a terrifying wave of biological terror to the battlefield. This diversity ensures that no two campaigns are ever quite the same, and mastering each faction’s strengths is part of the fun.

Engaging Campaign and Replayability

The campaign is full of engaging, story-driven missions that gradually ramp up the difficulty, keeping you on your toes. Each mission feels like a battle for the survival of Armageddon, and the branching storylines allow for multiple outcomes, giving the game substantial replay value. The game's challenge mode and multiplayer aspects further extend the gameplay, ensuring that even after completing the story, there's always something new to explore.

Conclusion

Warhammer 40K: Armageddon is a deeply engaging and well-crafted strategy game that stands out in the genre. With its rich lore, intricate tactical gameplay, and immersive atmosphere, it delivers a captivating experience for both Warhammer fans and strategy lovers alike. Whether you’re commanding the Imperium’s finest or leading the alien hordes to victory, Armageddon offers hours of tactical brilliance and satisfying combat. A must-play for fans of the franchise and strategy enthusiasts.
Rating: ★★★★★ (5/5)

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
A fun game to be sure, this title is a prime offering for individuals that want to dip their toes into TBS fare.  While not worth the outrageous offering price of 40 dollars, the game is worth purchasing if you can find the game on a hefty Steam sale.  Unfortunately, the game lacks the deep, rich tactical combat that it purports to have and, instead, exists as an annotated view of the the tactical/wargamer genre.  Warhammer 40K fans will be delighted at the amount of units but most individuals would probably be better served purchasing the game on the iPad.  The game runs just as well, controls just as fluidly, costs less and its difficulty level better matches a portable/mobility environment.

In fact, had the game not shipped on iOS almost a full year after I purchased it on Steam, I'd never have bought it for PC.  Weapons are said to affect different units in different ways but this is wholly untrue most of the time.  What's truly disappointing is paying 40 dollars for this game, which lacks any appreciable cut scenes, fleshed out story, unit animations, complex combat system or pervasive mulitplayer/modding community and yet experiencing Slitherine's absolute gall at asking for 20 dollars a scant year later for what amounts to a small expansion.  Seriously? You just charged us the price of a triple A game, despite obviously providing a much simpler experience and you can't give us some goodies and support before you hit us up again for money? 

Worth a look.

7/10.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
Pretty simple turnbased game similar to Panzer corps but with a Warhammer 40K skin. It's fun enough, I wouldn't say it's super immersive and there's unfortunately no satisfying explosions when enemies die but there's a TON of units to use, and the game encourages you to use a mix of units. Scenarios aren't bad but they're nothing to write home either, very forgettable. There are dialogue choices and I have no idea if it effects anything I think you can choose certain scenarios to do over others.
The sound effects are pretty cool, there's also range for each weapons and a single unit can have multiple weapons and there is some depth to combat where certain terrain has cover, certain units have assault trait to ignore cover at close range, some weapons ignore armor % some are better against unarmored targets etc. it's all very intuitive. 

Overall I'd give it a 6.5/10 pick it up if you're starving for 40K content or a  massive fan but it can definitely be overlooked. It's still a decent enough turn based strategy game

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
For only like 14 euro on steam sales for the whole collection with like 5 DLC camapigns, its a great game. At the time im writing this i have only finished the main campaign and im on 9th mission on the Salamander campaign.

If your playing the campaign for the first time i would just keep the fog off. As its hard enough to make it to the points in time. Also keep the undo on as it only allows you to undo movement if enemies can not reach the unit, saves you from a number of bad miss clicks.

I have not tried multiplayer but i have heard its janky with sending save files. If its like w40k sanctus reach its quite decent as you play your turn and when your done your opponent just opens the multiplayer menu and can jump in and play his turn whenever he wants. This makes it so you can have a number of games running at the same time and just play your turn when your ready for it. Good for people that do not have much time.

Positives

-Its a warhammer 40k styled turn based wargame.

-The larger scale battles compared to most other turn based strategy games is fun. Many of the missions more or less want you to make more than one combat group to send on different missions at the same time due to the turn limit. You would be to short on time if you moved the whole army around to one objective at a time. I like this as it makes you have to look at what your combat units needs to get to and capture the objectives in time. There is often enemies blocking the direct route that you can sometimes avoid to keep losses down and sometimes get to the objective faster. Using cover and line of sight blocks to keep units safe.

-You can disband units to get resources back whenever you want, as long as they are in reserve or you have not used attacks/movement that turn. This makes you able to quickly and easily change your army and deploy a new combat group to send on a objective.

Negatives
-You dont get to know the turn limit until after you deployed so you do not know how much time you got to capture the objectives until after deployment. Making it harder to know if you need faster movement or not.

-The game only got shooting effects no other animations. Making it look very cheaply made. Then along with a number of visual bugs and lacking voices for the mission briefings. This is quite horrible when you consider the price of the game when its not on sales being about 70 euro(Including the DLC campaigns).

-Attacked units always attack back if they got the range and if there is a blocking object they need the indirect trait to fire over it. This makes it so units can never really do a  hit and run attack. You always need a unit that is much stronger/much better armor/in a terrain advantage/out ranges the target. Making light vehicles, flying and scout units just unable to attack most things without horrible losses.

-The unit limit makes cheaper units somewhat useless. To the point where you end up not using hive militia or steel legion infantry units even tho it is quite useful to have fodder units to soak attacks. As fast as you can get Ogryns you start using them as your infantry units other than the command unit. Which is more or less vital to keep your units going.

-The game lack some things that other turn based w40k games got like splitting weapon fire between different targets, grenades, abilities and mounting/dismounting transports. 
Instead of transports some units can be bought with a transport so they get a option to become a transport or to travel that little extra range as a transport. If the unit dies the transports die as well.

Bugs

-Its has a number of visual bugs. 
After playing for a while it can get some odd visual bug in the mission briefings the background can become some rainbow/blue colored background and seen the same bug on some structure icon in one mission. Looked like it could been the faction flag for the faction captured the building that bugged out.

-Some units weapon mounting locations on the unit buy page is either missing the weapons or showing the wrong ones.(Salamanders predator tank should have 2 heavy bolters but there is non on the icon. Salmander Centurion with gravcanons got flamers on the icon)

Other bugs/design choices
-Some units you can clearly see should have 2 of the guns like the Centurions only got a single gun. Only the heavy bolter version got 2. This makes the units a lot weaker than it should be. There was also a Lemen russ tank that had plasma canons on the sides but only got one. Cant tell if these things is some form of balance or just some miss. The fire drake terminators also missing their powerfist attack.

-There is a limitation on weapons with how units in the game is made. Where if you got a unit which should have one special weapon in the squad everyone in the unit gets it. Since the unit is just whats shown on its stats times the number of entities in the unit. For example there is a Tactical squad(20 guys) equipped with a bolter and tactical squad(15 guys) with Bolter and graviton gun. Which makes it so those 15 got 2 main weapons instead of one just having a different gun. Would be much nicer if the units was either squads or platoons with a number of soldiers and weapon teams.


I feel like i have written way to much so im just gonna leave that here. Hopefully this helps people^^

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
While I enjoyed the game for the time being, I cannot recommend this game at the current price. Buy only if you are a fan of hex strategy AND Warhammer 40k.
The game design is quite simple - perhaps too simple - and after a few hours you have seen most of it. There are a number of different units to choose from, but you will mostly stick to the few worthwhile. For example in terms of armament, the available tanks are very similar anyway. Who needs 30 different tank loadouts? I would have liked to see an exciting upgrade and progression system, but each tank system only has one or two variations. You want a bulldozer blade or extra armor on that specific tank? Tough luck! It's not available.
The point requisition system for upgrading is extremely flawed anyway. Sometimes you get no points at all, which makes the game very frustrating for new players. This brings me to the role of bullet sponges: Basic infantry is dirt cheap and extremely viable because you can just replenish your squads in the middle of a battle. Your Steel Legion riflemen will just grow out of the ground like Orks, I guess. Get them into proper cover and they are nearly indestructible. Like a mindless Ork, the enemy will waste most of their fire on your "not so squishy" infantry. 
Speaking of the (non-existent) AI. Enemy units sometimes appear out of nowhere because the scenario says so. On the campaign map, enemy units sit on terrain tiles waiting for you to shoot them. Other times, they are heavily scripted and will B-line towards you after you enter a certain hex. If enemy units are weakened and broken enough, they will just sit around and cause traffic jams in defense missions. The enemy will never try to rescue units or retreat unless scripted to do so. Figuring out the "AI" is your main learning curve, and that process is not really fun.
Graphics are fine for me, but way too little effort for a $39 game. There are hardly any animations. Units don't look different when wounded or damaged. The battle map remains the same even after a fierce battle - cover cannot be created or destroyed.
You'll get a few lines of dialog between missions, but visually it's extremely uneventful. There's no map showing lines of control or where you're going in the next mission. It is the lack of such details and the (DLC) pricing model that make the game feel like an outrageous cash grab.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
There is no AI in this game, only heavily scripted missions. Enemy units which are not scripted for attack will just do absolutely nothing. They will let you shoot them and they will just stand there and take it.  

The main point of this game is to "guess" which units are scripted and will show minimal level of intelligence and which units are dead meat. Somehow they tried to make up for the lack of AI with very tight mission round counter. 

And even if the AI was working, the combat mechanics are so strange. This unlimited retaliation system just forces you into kind of one strategy: Shoot from distance until unit is so weak that retaliation does not matter. 

Armour does not really feel armoured at all as the lowest level gretchins can penetrate your Leman Russ.

Unit variety is not really there as many units are just plainly useless and you will stick to the ones that are not broken.

I like 40K and I like turn base strategies with 2d graphics, I was the ideal target audience and despite of that the game was real disappointment.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
Having now finished the campaign of the core game (depicting the events of the 2nd War for Armageddon), can now say it was a fun romp.

I must've played the first 10 missions of the campaign a thousand times before actually sitting down and committing to a full playthrough, and I am glad I did.

Game plays as a hex based 2D strategy game, putting you in command of the Steel Legion up against the invading Orks. Eventually over time you will end up commanding Space Marines of the Blood Angels, Ultramarines and Salamanders, not to mention Titan Legions and hero units such as Yarrick, Typhon and famous chapter masters such as Dante.

You build your army over time, taking units with you from mission to mission, who earn experience as you go (but if they die, they are gone forever, to be replaced with a new rookie unit of your choice), and the sheer amount of choice you have is insane. It's clear the devs have represented every unit ever mentioned in the lore (whether they have a tabletop version or not) and as such you can field some pretty exotic units along side the stuff you'd see in any 40k force.

The game is based on the Panzer Corp games, and although the game engine is newer than Panzer Corp 1, resulting in high resolutions and sharper graphics, it's also a "diet" version of that game, doing away with bonuses for encirclement or mass attacks for example. No ammo to worry about, or units retreating like you get in the WW2 version of the game.

Instead the strategy is mostly a rock paper scissors affair, ensuring your anti tank units are fighting tanks, anti infantry against infantry and your titans are taking on the Gargants etc etc.

There is a morale system, with units becoming less effective over time if constantly fighting, so rotating front line troops out of the thickest fighting to rest for a couple of turns is something you need to plan for, however as mentioned units won't break and retreat or anything like that. As I say, it's a "diet" hex based wargame, compared to the game it is based on. I think it works regardless, but worth mentioning, especially if you have played Panzer Corps before.

Although the graphics are fully 2D, the units themselves are rendered in sharp detail and it looks great in my opinion. There is little to no animation, with the only effects you'll see being the various weapon fire exchanged between units when ordered to attack. Each unit has a unique combat animation / effect though, so that's cool, with the expected weapons firing and exploding in the way you would think.

Lasgun beams are blue for some reason however, instead of the classic red, but there is a mod to change that if it bothers you 😃

All in all recommended fun, and there is plenty of DLC and a stand alone expansion called "Da Orks", which let's you play as the Orks instead, albeit in a different campaign against Yarrick and his forces.

So there we go, now onto the DLC!

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
Edit: I want to be clear that I haven't actually played for 157 hours. When you quit the game, the launcher stays open and that contributes toward your total time played.

I've been playing Armageddon on the Very Hard difficulty for some time now and I can sum up all of gameplay very simply: Get a unit with a lot of vision to reveal hexes, blast it at range with artillery, then close in with your melee guys to finish them off.  That's the whole game. Sometimes you might spice things up with a defense mission where you throw in "support units" that shoot when adjacent units are hit, but for the most part that's it. 

Moreover, the AI is bad. As a defender, if you get Orks onto a bridge, you can permanently lock them in place by getting them down to 1 HP and then just running out the clock. Your units act as a road block, preventing forward movement and the AI won't shuffle crippled unis to the back of the line, nor will they attack if it means certain death. HOWEVER, the AI is ALSO too passive when you're attacking. If the AI doesn't have vision on you, it won't do anything (as in it skips the AIs whole turn), even as you hammer at it with artillery turn after turn. 

It makes for a boring, drawn out experience. Armageddon doesn't feel like a test of your military tactics, it feels like a game of rock paper scissor. There's an absolutely colossal number of units, but most of them are useless or not as good as their alternative. If you bring the right units, you win every time. If you bring the wrong units, you lose. 

The only saving grace is the story moments in between, but even those get tiresome after a while because it boils down to "Commander, great job doing that thing, but the Orks are relentless, so we need you to go do a thing." It's that for 30 missions, just slightly rephrased each time.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
This game has what one other reviewer called an "old-fashioned aesthetic." By 2022 standards, I suppose that this is true. The maps can be a little bit drab and the appearance of the units in this game won't have you writing home in excitement to your mother. What they are is perfectly serviceable and decent - if you like this kind of game. This game is very much a spiritual successor of the original Panzer General/Pacific General/Allied General and, perhaps most of all, Warhammer 40,000: Rites of War. If you loved Rites of War back in the day and still enjoy play turn-based war games of the beer-and-pretzel variety, you'll have a lot to love here. There are a lot of similarities between that game and this. Based on what I remember from that game, however, combat resolution is somewhat more complicated in this game and is different, in fact, from contemporary superficially similar games like Slitherine's Order of Battle or Panzer Corps. It bears spending a few minutes reading some of the helpful guides here on Steam or - even more so - going over to the Slitherine folders and reading some of the posts there regarding combat mechanics, army management, and so on.

This isn't the kind of game that will necessarily offer you infinite replay value in single-player mode - the AI is fairly programmatic and you won't find the AI engaged in truly novel behavior if you replay any of the missions. On the basic, normal setting, the AI is not terribly difficult once you learn how to manage this games particular version of combined arms, but remains, in my estimation, entertainingly capable of punishing the careless player. Nevertheless, as I wrap up the first campaign, I find that this game hits the marks of what I was really hoping for:  a successor game to Rites of War, which I really enjoyed.

One caveat: if you are a true tabletop Warhammer 40k lover, you might find yourself puzzled by how certain units function in relationship to each other (which is to say, they don't work at all as they do on the table). This doesn't bother me, but I also knew what I was getting into.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
tl;dr: Would only recommend bought at reduced price and/or if you're nostalgic about Warhamemr Epic 40k or similar. However, I'm only in the first third of the game for now.

Edit: Change to reccomend, it gets better, story picks up a bit, feels like the battle goals varies more now.

I bought it on the cheap (I think 10€...?), even so I think it's decent at best. A lot of info seems to not be available. Having played the turtorial a lot is not explained, and I even find myself reading through the pdf manual to figure some of the stats out, but the manual is certainly not up-to-date:
 - Under "Examples of weapon statistics" a table is shown with some weapons having a range of 0, saying this means it can only attack units next to it. This is how range 1 functions. 
 - "Attacks per turn" is a stat, but it doesn't make a lot of sense. In-game a unit can only attack once, if it's for retaliating/firing back when attacked, it is my experience a unit can do so an infinite amount of times. So how does "ammo" and "attacks per turn" actually functino in the game? It seems to in-game be that "ammo" = amounts of shot per attack, it is as if these two stats in the manual have been merged in-game. It seems weird to call it "ammo" but it is nto somethign that can seemingly run out.
 - There are no aerial units in the manual, all other unit types are explained, but this, more unique unit type, is not.

For some games, learning by doing works great. In this sort of game it seems odd, more of a mistaken omision. 
What exactly does gaining XP do? It says it increases attacking and a smaller bonus to defence, however it does not explain in-game what it does. Seems weird it is not info that jsut pops up by hovering over the XP, plus the manual is a bit untrustworthy, obviously, so is that even what it does?

For the gameplay itself I think it gets a bit repetitive, but it's alright. It seems to be a bit of a shame you can do nothing to regain requisition points mid-fight, such as holding points, or defeating important targets. It is fun to experience some of the 1997 Final Liberation: Warhammer Epic 40,000, it's nostalgic in that way. However I'm missing the world campaign map from that game in this game. I miss the yanky and campy live action videos.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
TL;DR: Despite its simple presentation this is an addictive turn based tactics game with surprising depth.  Between all the different unit types to the game's mechanics to the different scenarios I was drawn into this game and couldn't let go.  Despite all of this, I think the base game is worth $20 at most.  If hex-based turn based tactics and WH40K are your thing then you should check this game out.

Gameplay: On the surface it appears simple, with each unit getting one move and attack action.  Deeper then that are all the different stats, weapons systems and tile types units have to deal with.  This means there is a good variety of units, with some being more versatile and others being more specialized.  Each unit can have up to three weapons, which can be useful in different situations or enhance a specific role.  You also have to consider "strength" (how many units is in a stack, usually ork units have higher strength).  Units can be carried over from one scenario to the next (if they survive) and increase in experience.  This makes them perform better.  You can also "upgrade" a unit, which allows them to be swapped with another valid unit.  It has to be the same type of the same faction though.  There are hundreds of units across five factions, but most of these are upgrade or sidegrade options to a standard unit.  Scenarios can have one objective or changing objectives, usually its to capture or defend hexes, move here or kill this.  The game is difficult, even on normal difficulty.  Expect to get spanked until you understand how the game works.

Content:  The base game has a 5 scenario tutorial, 30 scenario main campaign and a handful of one-off scenarios.  Each scenario takes 15-60 minutes to complete, depending on the size and the objective.  Main campaign took me 25 hours to complete, but that includes the tutorial plus time spend reading the manual and experimenting with army builds.  The main campaign is fully voice acted and has dialogue during missions too.  There are a good amount of twists and turns to keep you interested.  One issue is that by the 3rd act the game looses its steam.  Most of the scenarios are "capture this" with no twists and the orks become passive, save for a couple scenarios.  Also, by the 3rd act, all of the units are unlocked so there are no more surprises in that regard.  Otherwise the campaign is great with good sized fights and will keep you engaged.

Presentation:  Graphics look like an HD remake of a 90's game.  Some people hate it but I love it, they did a good job.  There are no unit animations but there are good and distinct attack effects based on the type of attack.  This makes it easy to discern what attack is doing what.  It seems goofy at first to see models slide around the map but it did not impede my enjoyment since it helps to save time.  Music is good, more like background ambient and orchestral pieces that set a meditative mood, which fits with the visual presentation.  Environments look detailed but my only complaint is that there isn't much variety in the maps in the base game.  There are a handful of scenarios in the jungle and one or two inside a hive city but otherwise its mainly out in the wastelands.

Performance:  On my budget desktop this game ran smooth without issue.  I didn't experience any glitches save for one weird visual glitch in between missions.  I run on windows 10 and haven't had any issues launching the game.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon

I can only assume Slitherine have at this point given up and make the game engine and resources for the modders and throw out a half baked beta safe in the knowledge the modders will pick up the pieces and sort it out...its the only explanation that makes sense to me when you see some of the EPIC and basic errors in the game.
Don`t bother looking at this base game..go look at the mods available and make your decision on buying it based on if there are any mods you like as the base game is an abysmal joke.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
An unfinished game that I would only recommend to die-hard 40k fans when on sale. Before buying, skim through this great guide by Cuddles to get an idea of how half-baked this game's systems are, and then decide if you still want it: 

https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=1089864462

Sure it could be enjoyable if you're looking for just any sort of strategy experience with a 40k skin... But if you're really bothered by [u]nonsensical design choices[/u] (there is no mechanic for "blasts", basic infantry can shred aircraft) [u]incomplete systems[/u] (none of the aircraft in the game are actual "flyers" even though the movement type exists in the game files. They "hover" and they can't hover over cliffs or mountains. The manual if full of references to other cut mechanics) and [u]not being able to see basic information[/u] in game (how much does this terrain impair my movement? why does my weapon do this much damage? what is my morale doing to my unit?) then i would give this game a pass. It has potential, and maybe a big fan patch could make it a much better game, but as far as I know there's no such magic bullet.

Tactically it's a challenge and I like that. Not too hard, not too easy. Why more people haven't picked up this game title is beyond me. For being Warhammer, and with Warhammer having a not so great reputation on Steam, this is a really good 40K tactical game. So from my point of view, you should pick up this game and have fun.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
Panzer Generals/Corps retarded cousin.

This is not necessarily a bad game, but if you're looking for strategic depth you won't find it here. There's also a story in there somewhere, and some dialogue options, which apart from the case when you're choosing a mission, they don't seem to do anything.

The UI is terrible when it comes to managing your units, I found it really hard to figure out which units are the best because there's no way to sort them and you have to hover over attributes to find out what they are which is really annoying. The icon chosen for the upgrade button doesn't make any sense. Also I'm not exactly sure what unit experience does or how it's supposed to make the unit better. You can't overpower a unit like in the panzer games.

There's also a resource that doesn't do anything. Just accumulates and that's it. No idea what it's for.

On the battle map you can't tell if a unit is in cover or if it has any buff or debuffs. Damage estimates are confusing. I don't get the point of some unit types. Air units are not really air units, they're more like hover; they can't stack over another unit, they can't go over hills/mountains, they have the same range as a ground unit. Infantry, tanks and artillery is all I'm using.

If you're a W40k fan sure scratches an itch, but I'd only get it heavily discounted like I did. I'ts just not worth the full price.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
[i]"He was an avalanche from an unexpected quarter. He was a thunderbolt from a clear sky."[/i]
— Commissar Yarrick on Ghazghkull Mag Uruk Thraka

[h1]INTRODUCTION[/h1] 
[b]"Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon"[/b] takes place during the [b]Second War for Armageddon[/b], and is the second of three massive conflicts between the [b]Imperium of Man[/b] and the Ork Warboss, [b]Ghazghkull Mag Uruk Thraka[/b] that took place between 941.M41 through 943.M41. The war was fought on the Hive World of Armageddon in the Segmentum Solar, where Ghazghkull Thraka launched an [b]Ork WAAAGH![/b] consisting of five separate Ork tribes in an attempt to seize the planet for themselves.

Why is Armageddon so important to the Orks? It was once the planet of Ullanor, an Ork stronghold world that was initially crushed by Primarch Horus Lupercal, where he received the title Warmaster from the Emperor - opening the path to Lorgar's treacherous corruption of Horus, the Emperor's favoured son. 
It once again became the 'homeworld' of sorts for five enormous Ork "Beasts" in M32 that launched an invasion of Terra, and saw the original Imperial Fists wiped out to a man (the chapter was rebuilt with volunteers from successor chapters). 
After the Ork's defeat, Ullanor was said to be destroyed, but was instead ransacked by the Adeptus Mechanicus, transported to the Segmentum Solar using Ork technology and renamed Armageddon. 

But shhh! The Imperium doesn't know this.

Three Space Marine Chapters were involved in the second war, including: the Blood Angels (under Lord Commander Dante), the Salamanders and the Ultramarines, along with dozens of regiments of the Astra Militarum, as well as a large contingent of Inquisition forces from the Ordo Xenos composed of Squats. A notable effect of this invasion was that after the war, Sebastion Yarrick, the "Hero of Hades Hive" (a Commissar of the Officio Prefectus) attempted to follow through on his oath, that he would not rest until the day Ghazghkull Mag Uruk Thraka was hunted down and killed. He set off in pursuit of his nemesis in a game of cat-and-mouse that lasted for nearly ten standard years. Ghazghkull fled before Yarrick and a company of Mordian Iron Guard. The Ork Warboss protected what remained of his forces, only committing to rearguard actions when necessary. Yarrick attempted to break the deadlock by forcing his opponent down on the once-Imperial planet of Golgotha. What happened next....*spoilers*.

This is all covered in the final DLC [b]"Golgotha"[/b] (a very challenging set of scenarios where you the player fight at company level with core units you must look after well), but is essential to complete the tale.

The Second War for Armageddon saw also the transformation of the Librarian Brother Calistarius, presumed dead after falling to the Black Rage (or Red Thirst) and "dying" under the rubble of a cathedral. Rising anew after battling the Black Rage for seven days and seeing an image of Sanguinius raising him high, Calistarius was no more. In his stead stood Mephiston, the Lord of Death, second now only to Lord Commander Dante and seen as Dante's eventual successor.

But, that is a tale for another time.

[b]NOTE:[/b] Due to the fact that the history of the First War for Armageddon against Chaos has been entirely suppressed by the actions of the Inquisition, most of the Imperium, and the citizens of Armageddon itself, considered this Second War to be the First War for Armageddon, rather than the second.  


[h1]REVIEW[/h1] 
Based upon the now ageing [b]"Panzer Corps"[/b] engine, the game is still enthralling as you get to choose, from Act 2 on whether you keep your initial core of Astra Militarum Steel Legion under your command, via Commissar Sebastion Yarrick of the Officio Prefectus (aka. "The Old Man"), or if you wish to include Blood Angels and Ultramarines (Salamanders come in the Third Acts). The vast armouries under the overall command of Lord Commander Dante of the Blood Angels are open to the player, including all the Adeptus Astartes have to offer, and the Steel Legion ranks including Titans later in the game. 

It is a Turn-Based Strategy game, where the goal on each map is to seize objectives and wipe out your opponent, or hold your objectives against waves of Ork tribal warriors and their ricketty though deadly machines. 
You the player, are "The Commander" tasked with increasingly important missions, your exploits growing in story panels between missions, from critiques by the traitor Governor von Straub to exhortations to hold vital sites by Lord Commander Dante, to aggressively snatching Ork holdings by Yarrick. Whew!

It could be thought that the maps in thee game are quite ugly and make the game look bland of the ugliness of the maps, but this is Armageddon, a burnt and blistered hive world covered in irradiated deserts and equatorial jungles. It is ugly yes, but also fitting. The equatorial jungle maps do break it up somewhat, though.

Some of the DLC's add such units as the Space Marines separately with their own mini campaigns, while others such as [b]"Ork Hunters"[/b] introduce missions set in the jungles following on from the main campaign, that is broken into Acts. All of the DLC are expansions to the main game, adding new missions and units as mentioned and the game really is incomplete without them all. For the full experience, the Second Battle for Armageddon where Yarrick made his name and the legend was born you need the whole package. While you might think, especially early on where you only have Steel Legion forces (the Astra Militarum based on Armageddon) that the battles are the same, and 'grindy', once the Space Marines land and you get to include Ultramarines and Blood Angels (the sons of Vulkan appear and have their own mini campaign) the once mighty and indestructible Ork force start to whither. 
Send a Blood Angel Death Company (those taken by the Black Rage) force at a Morkanaut and see what happens. The entire Armouries of the Adeptus Astartes are open to you the player.
Aside from the Adeptus Astartes and the Steel Legion troopers, you also have Titans, under the command of Princeps Prime Kurtiz Mannheim, the commander of the [b]Adeptus Mechanicus' Legio Metalica Titan Legion[/b] that was temporarily stationed on Armageddon. 
Basically you have it all. It is a very long, and often very challenging war especially on the higher difficulty settings.

[h1]CONCLUSION[/h1] 
It is expected that there will be a [b]"W40K: Armageddon 2"[/b] based on the [b]"Panzer Corps 2"[/b] engine, but if it will just be a rehash of this war or go onto the Third War for Armageddon remains to be seen. My vote is for the Third War as you have The Steel Legion, Salamanders, Space Wolves, Black Templars, Celestial Lions, Sisters of Our Martyred Lady and Titans, the war made famous in the Black Library novel and fan YouTube video series of the same name, [b]"Helsreach"[/b].

Despite it's ageing engine, drab maps reminiscent of [b]"THH: Battle of Tallarn[/b], and requirement for much time investment to get the best experience, [b]'W40K: Armageddon"[/b] is an essential purchase for all [b]Warhammer 40,000[/b] fans and those who wish to explore this incredible war that led to Yarrick ("The Old Man") becoming a legend and the only Commissar that all Space Marines truly respect.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
One of the best round based 40K games out there ever.

At the beginning, I hated that the unit sprites only have six drawn directions (west, east, nw, ne, sw, se), but that fades away quick. You get used to it and discover that it is enough to depict what is going on on the battlefield.

The game is more big and complex than you can ever extract out of the store page media. A diamond of the void.

There is a magnificent rule system which for shooting around takes into account both the statistics of the plattform (e.g. accuracy) and statistics of the weapon installed on that particular version of what you have. (Leman Russ with its felt thousands of variations.) Also, there are very interesting weapon statistics which you have to consider wisely, because one weapon can have superior damage, but then is fired only once per attack animation, whereas another weapon could be fired 6 times instead, enabling to apply its properties multiple times per attack on the target. There also is a value which tells you the ranged accuracy loss per hexfield distance to target. Very cool stuff here! An interesting unit "health" system enabling units to recover on the battlefield without losing your prestige, but you waste some turns. I love the additional weapon properties like "terror" which causes a weapon (e.g. most flamers) to cause double morale damage on the enemy. The game manages well to introduce you to the vast and evil armament of your beloved imperial units.

I dislike how weak regular tanks are sometimes when they are chopped in half by assault units. But that is WH40K style as I experienced it throughout many a 40K game. The units themselves have a good presence and nice sprites. It is pleasing to own and control them. You can individually alter the general speed of movement and shooting to create the battle dynamics you love. I always decrease both to enjoy the unit combat advance and especially the shooting, which is hilarious when the platform uses three different weapon systems installed. (e.g. Leman Russes, Land Raiders; or even Terminator Squads can have three: e.g. when going into hand to hand combat they in succession use Heavy Bolters, Assault Cannons and Power Fists.)

In this game I can do what I most like about round based games: I perfectly re-live what every single unit experiences on the battle field like it was an RPG. It is really cool.

Yes, the Orks are dumb and on the base the game is just about controlling the Ork waves without getting too slaughtered and pumping as much preferably distanced damage into them as you can. (Well that doesn't mean I don't like Terminator Assault Squads.) Also, the most "ambushes" are scripted or due to initial enemy unit placement and distance like a flyer placed behind you on the edge of the map: It will ambush you from behind the next turns, yes.

You can implement interesting play styles and unit aquisition strategies to improve your game experience after you already played through the game. You will learn how to utilize the all in all fragile units best and get used to it. Then you can pull the most power out of this game. For instance every Terminator squad is not only an infantry unit, but a not to underestimate tank unit when they have a regular Land Raider as integrated transport. You can also switch transport for a unit just to have it in armor during the Ork turn, possibly then being able to adequately retaliate in a ranged manner as opposed to its former infantry state. (e.g. Termiz Assault Squad)

There are big things going on on the battlefield. The terrain is wide and big. There are cities and jungles. You have Titan Legion units and Superheavies running around and causing utter destruction.

I perceived the game intro movie very cheap at the beginning, when Horst tells you what is going on in the Imperium and what Armageddon is. But by now, I found it a very powerful speech beautifully and brutally underlined by cool moving pictures and nice sounds. Sometimes I just open the intro movie file and tremble under its minimalistic cinematic power =D The sounds in this game absolutely rule. Have a unit with much installed weapons fire around and hear what I mean. Sometimes when the Orcs attack and imperial retaliations take place the battle, although serial, looks and feels almost like parallel fighting. You can enjoy this so much.

You can zoom in and out the map as you wish very big distance. Some games even fail hard in this matter.

This game is a good container to let go your deep and dark desire to serve the emperor. Just wait for the moment when first time Space Marines arrive to lend you a hand in battle. I could just cry because I love these guys and my own units so much and how glorious it was the Commissar held out so long until imperial reinforcements Astartes arrived. Big brain sex. Wonderful. Between missions you can make decisions sometimes and you hear the leading characters of the game talk. This brings you nearer to the cause and bolsters your strength!

There is much to like here and I surely forgot things to tell you. But this is THE ONLY WH40K hexfield game out there. This is what other round based WH40K games must compare to. The developers fulfilled the will of the emperor :3 He guided their coding keyboard hands.

Fear not this software product, for it will help you to achive enlightment!

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
Summary
-----
As a wargame, Armageddon is mediocre at best. The scenarios are interesting and challenging, but it uses a lot of wargame tropes that I do not like. If I were rating Armageddon solely on its merits as a wargame, I probably would not give it a thumbs up. As a Warhammer 40k game…well, Armageddon is definitely not 40k…but it does an excellent job of making you feel like you are playing 40k. And for that, the God-Emperor can forgive many sins.
-----

If you are a Warhammer 40k fan, Armageddon will scratch that itch. The game is immersed in the lore and you will interact with some legendary 40k icons. The unit images are surprisingly detailed for a mid-low graphics game, and they are very true to the setting. Playing Armageddon [i]feels[/i] like 40k.

Great! Unfortunately, Armageddon is definitely not 40k. Matchups don’t play out correctly…a heavy bolter devastator squad will take real damage fighting Gretchen, lascannons are not even remotely good as anti-armor weapons (in fact, they are not even remotely good), and so on. Armageddon rebalanced the units, modified weapons, changed stats…really nothing is [i]actually[/i] 40k except for the names, pictures, and story. That’s ok, I guess. Don’t look too closely, don’t think too hard, and you can fool yourself into thinking you are playing 40k.

I have to give respect to Armageddon for figuring out a way to effectively put Gretchen, Space Marines, Deff Dreads, Lehman Russ tanks, Baneblade Super Tanks, and Ork Gargants on the same battlefield. That isn’t easy, and the game does an ok job at this challenging task. 

As a wargame, I am much more split on how I feel. Scenarios are generally challenging and require good tactical choices. A good understanding of your units and their abilities also make a big difference in the scenario outcome. As a self-styled strategy veteran, I took a sound thrashing when my first playthrough was on Challenging (one step up from Normal).

Despite being good at some core requirements for a wargame, Armageddon has some notable flaws. This is one of those games where if you do really well, the next scenario will be easier. If you get clobbered, then you have no units for the next scenario. I hope you had fun because that’s the end of your campaign…even if you managed to win the scenario. No, seriously…don’t end the scenario. If you start up the next one you are stuck with your trash heap of broken units as the basis for your army for the rest of the campaign. I personally don’t like this style of game as you are actively discouraged from challenging yourself at the most interesting moments. A hard fought, narrow victory is much more satisfying than a crushing success.

Armageddon also fails the basic test for a high-quality wargame. Some units are just better than others, and you can stomp most scenarios with a nonsense army made up of only 2 or 3 kinds of units. Think about the lore as you order up 50 new Dreadnaughts…it will make you laugh and it will make you cry.

Armageddon is also one of those games where part of the difficulty is “have you played this scenario before?” Lower difficulties give you the extra turns you need to figure out how to win on a map. Higher difficulties require you to use resources optimally against things that start off in the fog of war. I did not buy this to play a game of “How well do you remember the map?”

There are also some real mechanical headscratchers…like units that can only see 2 hexes away but can accurately shot at 3 (this is actually quite common)…like, wtf? Oh, and vision is THE most important stat. If you can see and shoot 3 hexes, and the enemy can only see and shoot 2, then you can just sit and mow them down. They don’t counterattack, they don’t move, they just sit and take it. As soon as they see you, though, then suddenly they get ticked-off and come after you.

The DLCs offer some very interesting scenarios, and many of them put you in situations where you need a larger variety of units to succeed. Don’t expect much story in the DLCs, but they usually make a worthy addition, if you want more Armageddon.

With all that said, I will repeat the most important point, it felt like I was playing 40k, and that was enough for me to enjoy the game.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
While as it is, it is another panzer corp-like hexen game Slitherine Ltd. is renowned for. However, the basic mechanics of those classic hex turn based strategy games blend well with the utter chaos of the 41st millennium. There are difficulty spikes and times where I rolled my eyes because of dice rolls or ridiculous gretchin mob attacks, but I feel that this has laid the foundation for the possibility of future games in this concept be delivered outside the Armageddon scope. The visuals and audio were satisfactory, the available units were excellent, but I never found the scale where I really wanted it to be. Grand battles felt like skirmishes. 

Fortunately there was enough mission variety in the grand campaign, with hero units, protection of refugees or defence missions to keep things different from between capturing victory hexes over and over.  

If you're a w40k fan, and tolerant of light strategy, I would recommend this game. I believe there is an active mod community too.

review: I really like the game, but I'd like it better on mobile. It's fully 2d, doesn't take up a ton of space, and the turn-based-point-and-click nature of tactics games lend themselves really well to touch screens. The tactical depth is there, but the sprites are just "ok" and they don't have many animations. Now I don't care about graphics as much as game play, and the game play is great, but if I'm playing on PC, I have a ton of choices with both the tactical depth AND graphics. That is something much less of a concern on mobile devices, because even though they are capable of decent graphics, 3d tends to drain battery; so lower fidelity 2d is more desirable. I'd totally buy this game again on mobile.

However, this game has got bugs, nothing game breaking, but I experienced a reaccuring issue in which the background in dialogue sections would turn green, as if it was missing the image file, also on 2 occasions, some voice lines didn't play, nothing major and despite these issues, the game is overall great BUT, buy this on sale because the standard price is INSANE for a game that has the same graphical quality as sandpaper

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
It seems all too simple, at first.  It looks like the lovechild of the 20+ year old "40K Final Liberation" and the nearly as ancient "40K Rites of War" and given the massive advances over a couple of decades, this melding *should not* work. Not in the least.

But, DAMN, does it ever work!  The objectives lend them selves to deep strategic warfare, the enemy AI is more than capable of going toe-to-virtual-toes with you, and --best of all-- the upgrade option for troops, vehicles, and even *ahem* machines that fly, are intuitively integrated.  Also a real joy after you get over the initial shock, is the massive number of individual unit stats...for every possible variable.  It would be enough to make a neck-bearded Avalon Hill purist cry...if it weren't for the fact that they are carefully crafted to ALL have weight, purpose, and their inherent specific uses.  I picked up all of the symbols and uses of the stats, with zero manual reading or guides, in my first time playing the game, and it took less than an hour until I had confident grasp of each, and was leading my troops into the thick of one of the many, varied battle types.

This game costs little, but it obviously had AAA-money levels of love in its concept, art, and game play design.  Pay "the little"   The return on investment is phenomenal!

Normal cost of the game is bit high for what it is, but it is a good game. Under discount? A must buy.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
A good game for fans of hex-based tactical simulation or 40k.

The art style is quite detailed and looks good. Armageddon really looks like a grim, war-torn environment. Of course, the environments do get rather samey over the course of the campaign.

As expected in such games, you use terrain to your advantage and figure out which units work against which. Damage is predicted quite accurately so you won't face many surprises (unless you scout badly).

The story is presented in a mature way, with believable characters (orks will of course be orks). You really feel like you're in a desperate war against the orks, and the war feels like an actual war with proper strategy being used.

Mission variety is pretty good in Act 1, in Act 2 there's less variety.

Once you get access to space marine units and the high end Steel Legion units, the dynamic of the campaign changes quite a bit and you feel way more powerful. At least on normal difficulty, Act 2 felt far less challenging, but after being bullied by the orks for the entire first half of the game, I can't say I minded it too much.

AI will focus fire exposed units quite hard, but is also very passive in missions where they're the defender (on normal difficulty).

There is a big variety of units, which doesn't mean all of them are viable, but it's fun to play around with regardless.

The game has decent replayability if you want to attempt a higher difficulty, or make different choices (the choices aren't super impactful except for a few times where you get branching missions, but still worth exploring if you're curious about the different outcomes).

Every mission has a certain amount of 'requisition' and units unlock at set times. You usually have more than enough requisition to fill out all your army slots, which doesn't incentivise using cheap troops to get a bigger army (though you could do it to have more requisition left to replenish your wounded troops, I suppose) so the extent to which you manage your army comes down to trying to maintain veterans and choosing the 'right' units.

There's a good amount of content here, towards the end it did get slightly repetitive, once the 'tech tree' had been fully explored, and started to drag a little, but perhaps that's because I binged it and it did keep me engrossed enough to carry it through to the end with no major breaks between sessions (a very rare thing for me).

A solid 7.5./10. I recommend picking it up on a sale.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
This game is greatly underrated. You'll find few Warhammer games that have such a coherent and immersive story campaign as Armageddon does. If you don't judge the game by it's 2D-visuals, you'll find a challenging strategy game that will keep you hooked, mission after mission. 

You'll interact with plenty well-written characters during the briefings and debriefings, make choices on which front to fight and build yourself an army to command against overwhelming foes. 

If you are a fan of Warhammer and/or strategy games, do yourself a favor and get it now!

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
+
Excellent turn based, hexagon, Panzer General style game. Based on Warhammer 40k, a sci-fi game with humans versus greenskin Orcs with lasers.
Campaign with very many, varied,  scenarios. Troops improve in quality. Limited resources.
Added campaigns for Space Marine super-soldiers from various Chapters. 
Tactics are vital.  Have you troops in trenches with a machine gun behind them. It will fire in support and pile up those dead greenies.
Varied storyline, choice branches. 
Many troop types, so you can replay campaign. For example play tank or infantry or Titan (mech) oriented, as you wish. Sniper spot far (arty shoots far!) but get eaten if they get too close.
Full text and voice scenes between scenarios. Pro voice actors. Pro artists and story (from WH40k).
Vary unit mix to win.  {E.g. I like basic infantry, but needed to upgrade them to Ogryns for city fights).
No bugs [except in mine a couple of the between scenario pictures were corrupt. Didn't damage game].
Choice of difficulty.
-
Vast horde worries. Lodsa Orkies. Kids will cry as their scattered forces are overrun by the green tide... Stay together and you can't get all the Objectives. Lose units a lot and they wont get experienced...
A bit pricey for an older game...
Not all troop types get used. (E.g. I didn't bother with aircraft.]
Game graphics are showing their age [compare old Panzer General to Order Of Battle].

+++ If you like it you can buy the Orc campaign with Thraka and his attack squigs! +++

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
TL;DR

For 37 euros this game is ridiculous. Buy it on sale, only if:

- the discount is at least 60%, imho 10 bucks is realistic for this game
- you like Panzer General and/or you are a Warhammer 40k fan
- you don't mind barebone graphics and sound
- you don't mind playing a game which feels like a veeeery budget title from all aspect

Update: I played this game for around 10 hours more and yep, it doesn't get any better. AI is still brain dead, if it moves units around (or does anything at all, apart of counter attacking), then it's scripted as fuck, like only attacks mission critical units, or when it's an invasion scenario. The only difficulty comes from two factors, one is that time to time you are totally drained of requisition points for a scenario or two and the second is that orcs are present on the maps in a much higher number and they have access to vastly superior units. Shame, this easily could have been a pretty good game, albeit it seems Slitherine/devs do nothing but churning out overpriced DLCs.


Wall of text

Ok, so I love turn based strategy, especially the Panzer/Fantasy/whatever General series and I like Warhammer 40k, but never played board / tabletop games, only read the books of Ian Watson. This game is basically a Panzer General clone, with very bad usability / quality of life value, but decent fun to play (except at that price, which is fucking insane for a 6 years old game, but Slitherine's favourite sport is charging an arm and a leg for a game, which doesn't worth that much).

I can't talk about the authenticity to the Wh40k lore, since I don't know it in detail, but it really scratch that Panzer General itch of mine. Although I have to say, you have to make big compromises at this game and the sad part is, the some of it could be easily patched.

Stuff which I particularly disliked (played on normal difficulty):

- AI is borderline to non-existent, I mean enemy units don't really do anything at all, unless it's scripted to do something  (e.g. you approach an enemy unit too close, trigger an event and such). It happens to an extent, that (for example) you can comfortably use snipers to wear down huge orc melee units, because they don't give a shit. While if the AI would be so gracious to move the orc melee units 3-4 tiles, they would easily mow down my snipers. But they don't, because of reasons. If you played Firaxis' Civ games, then I can say that AI there is something like a genius, compared to this. So I would say it's more of a puzzle game for me, rather than a strategy game. It's also impossible to call it a strategy game, as for the first couple of maps, those are way too small to apply any tactics at all.
- Some fluff/flavour text/lore/description for existing units would be appreciated, because if someone is not a Wh40k buff, then most of them don't mean shit to him. That's especially a part I don't understand why is it missing, as this doesn't require huge production value.
- There are unit slots, which I did and still call the laziest fucking possible way to balance a Panzer General like(ish) game. Still, at least one slot means one unit, not like Panzer Corps 2, where you have to sacrifice multiple slots even for infantry transport. Not a big comfort for me, though.
- Usability / quality of life is basically non-existent and/or the developers just didn't give shit. For example if the enemy moves it's units, you have to watch it (which is normal). But if enemy movement is covered by fog of war, you still have to wait for it, making moves. So for a large enemy army on move, it means you will stare at the screen for 10-20-whatever seconds, until stuff happens hidden by the fog of war. I mean WTF?!
- I don't know if this game adapts the tabletop rules per se, but some stuff is fucking annoying for someone who just likes TBS games, but doesn't know Wh40k games in details. Like air units can't occupy the same slots as ground units. It may make sense for enemy units (due to weapon range), but for friendly units, it's very annoying. Also, air units work an odd way, e.g. my gunship was taken down by a flamethrower unit. I mean da fuck, effectively a melee unit took down a flying unit?!
- Some missions are simply annoying and/or sloppily explained. For instance order is to attack and hold 4 victory hexes. Ok... then an enemy counter attack triggered and I get the order to move at least 4 units to the rightmost part of the map. Ok... but the original victory hexes are still there, and the mission information is not updated and still says to hold 4 victory hexes. What...the...fuck?! Am I supposed to move 4 units to the right and meanwhile hold the victory hexes, or move all units, but four minimum, or...? It turned out to be a full retreat, but it is really not told anywhere at all.
- Other mission briefing is like check crash sites for a pilot, but there are multiple ones, so I have to check all them. Mission information says I have to hold 2 of them, marked with flag. But there are like 10 or more crash sites, all of them marked with special hex symbol, two of them with flags. So what...the...fuck...?! Am I supposed to check all of them, then at the end hold two, or just focus on the two, or...? Oh, by the way it turned out that only the two ones matter with flags, the remaining god knows why are there.

Ok, I stop the rant, my point is that this game is on the market for 6 years, yet neither developers, nor Slitherine gave a shit about fixing obvious issues, but obviously, overpriced DLCs are out there as usual. Costing you over 60 euros for the whole shebang, which is the same amount, you will pay for a AAA game developed from like a 100 million USD, which fact makes me feel like this:

https://media.giphy.com/media/Vg0JstydL8HCg/giphy.gif

review:
I gave it a second try. I listened to the people who play Warhammer 40k. I tried the multi-player.  THAT was god awful. The rando that played it was not very sportsman like. The Warhammer 40K gaming community is HIGHLY toxic. Even for those who play a video game.

 After playing this video game with a toxic 40K community member, I decided to get rid of all 40K models/bock/ paraphernalia in the real world.  It is time to step away from Games Work Shop. When you compare this video game and GW, both have a high model count required for a game, or in a game. This is sort of a forced consumption of consumerism and materialism that is beyond unhealthy. I get the impression the high number of model counts is a mental illness like a hoarder. Event he high numbers in the video game fuel this.   

  We (myself and a pro-twitch streamer) evaluated it on a live stream of it. That went over like a dirty diaper on the lunch table.  Multiplayer was not an option. Viewership went from good, to noone. Top complaints were the color pallet being all brown, the bad animations and graphics, and the extreme boring game play.  The in game dialogue was pretty harsh and set off a TOXIC melt down with multiple people.  As one of the viewers put it, “I have a shitty life, I play (video) games to escape, I don’t want to listen to some game put me down, nah f**K that”. The game itself promoted the toxic culture by verbally abusing the player in the cut scenes. I felt in necessary to apologies for the game and for us deciding to play the game. Despite all the research we put into, nobody found it entertaining. We couldn't even convince ourselves to keep it going. 


The summation of the game is to pound infantry with artillery. The summation of the game is to pound infantry with artillery to ad nasuseam. 



Original review below


This is about the stupidest and most obnoxious game I have ever played.

I have reconsidered my original opinion, my new opinion is Games WorkShop (40K), and everything about Games WorkShop needs to go away.  The whole game model is just a tedious grind where you get more infantry to acts a shields while your armor beats down your opponents infantry.  This game, the rules and the whole concept is dated, like 1950s bad, dated.  No tactics, no planning, no thought beyond clink and point send a mad horde of infantry and armor to overpower your opponent, before the turn counter runs out.   Seriously, who the heck wins a war by throwing away armor and life, in reckless and needless fashions?  Games Work shop and all it products are the worst games imaginable for strategy.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
The only 40k game that lets you have 25,000+ models on the map over large scale distances.
Each icon on the map is made up of individual models.  For example a hex with a unit icon in it like the will have a number under it.  That number is how many models are apart of that group.  When an Icon attacks another icon, the models are all rolling for their shots.  So if you have  baneblade that has 2 under the icon, that means there is 2 models in that group. The models all have their own wounds, which you can see when you hover over the icon.  It is just like the table top.  Only you get to control so much more.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: This is an excellent wargame which balances interesting mechanics to model the the complexities of warfare in the 40k universe without becoming excessively complicated (like Gary Grigsby's War in the East/West). Each unit has a number of stats that determine their effectiveness in different situations: vision distance, initiative, armour, weapon damage, armour piercing, movement level/type etc. From, looking at the new 40k Gladius also from the publisher Slitherine I think I prefer this because the individual scenarios each with different objectives and maps seem to be more tactical with a deployment phase, with limited requisition points that carry over from mission to mission and are also required to reinforce your squads. Furthermore, the squads gain experience from their kills and loose it when some of the squad dies. Squads also carry over from mission to mission as long as they survive and within their unit category (Infantry, Vehicles, Walkers, Armour, Airpower, Titans) can be switched to different unit types as the mission requires. You therefore need to value the lives of your squads and learn to give them wise orders. All these mechanics, the huge unit variety along with varied routes within maps to complete objectives opens up a space for more strategic as well as tactical decision making. The Armageddon story is also interesting with good voice acting. I like the graphical style of the game which provides a pleasing form of abstraction like a board game. I wish they would add more campaigns, a skirmish mode different objective types against the AI and more races to the game. Like the Tyranid invasion of a Cadian Guard Jungle world. Adding Tau, Necrons, chaos Marines, Eldar etc would make the game even more awesome. Voice overs for the infantry would be good also 'For the Emperor!'.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
'Do you see that?' he asked quietly.

 

At first, only silence followed. Hesitantly, the Guard soldiers began to cast glances at each other, uncomfortable with the Chaplain's presence and confused by his behaviour. All eyes were on him now. Grimaldus aimed his mace out at the advancing hordes. Thousands. Tens of thousands. And only the very beginning.

 

'Do you see that?' he roared at the humans. the closest ranks flinched back from the mechanical bark that issued almost deafeningly loud from his skull helm.

 

'Answer me!'

 

He received several trembling nods. 'Yes, sir...' uttered a handful of them, the speakers faceless within the masses of their rebreather masks.

 

Grimaldus turned his back to the wasteland, already dark with the teeming, chaotic ranks of the enemy. At first, his helm emitted a low, vox-distorted chuckle. Within a few seconds, he was laughing, laughing up at the burning sky while aiming his crozius hammer at the enemy.

 

'Are you as insulted as I am? This is what they send against us?'

 

He turned back to the men, the laughter fading, but amused contempt filling his voice even through the inhumanising vocalisers of his helm.

 

'This is what they send? This rabble? We hold one of the mightiest cities on the face of the planet. The fury of its guns sends all skyborne enemies to the ground in flames. We stand united in our thousands - our weapons without number, our purity without question, and our hearts beating courage through our blood. And this is how they attack us?'

 

'Brothers and sisters... a legion of beggars and alien dregs wheezes its way across the plains. Forgive me when the moment comes that they whine and weep against our walls. Forgive me that I must order you to waste ammunition upon their worthless bodies.'

 

Grimaldus paused, lowering his weapons at last, turning his back on the invaders as if bored bu their very existence. His attention was focussed entirely upon the soldiers below him.

 

'I have heard many souls speak my name in whispers since I came to Helsreach. I ask you now: Do you know me?'

 

'Yes' several voices replied, several among the hundreds.

 

'Do you know me?' he bellowed at them over the firing of the wall guns.

 

'Yes!' a chorus answered now.

 

'I am Grimaldus of the Black Templars! A brother to the Steel Legions of this defiant world!'

 

A muted cheer greeted his words. It wasn't enough, not even close.

 

'Never again in life will your actions carry such consequences. Never again will you serve as you serve now. No duty will matter as much, and no glory will taste as ture. We are the defenders of Helsreach. On this day, we carve our legend in the flesh of every alien we slay. Will you stand with me?'

 

Now the cheers came in truth. They thundered in the air around him.

 

'Will you stand with me?'

 

Again, a roar.

 

'Sons ans daughters of the Imperium! our blood is the blood of heroes and martyrs! The xenos dare defile our sacred city? They dare tread the sacred soil of our world? We will throw their bodies from these walls when the final day dawns!'

 

A wave of noise crashed against his armour as they cheered. Grimaldus raised his war maul, aiming it to the embattled heavens.

 

'This is our city! This is our world! Say it! Say it! Cry it out so the bastards in orbit will hear our fury! Our city! Our world!'

 

'OUR CITY! OUR WORLD!'

 

Laughing again, Grimaldus turned to face the oncoming horde. '*Run, alien dogs! Come to me! Come to us all! Come die in blood and fire!'

 

'BLOOD AND FIRE!'

 

The Reclusiarch cut the air with his crozius, as if ordering his men forward. 'For the Templars! For the Legion! For Helsreach!'

 

'FOR HELSREACH!'

 

'Louder!'

 

'FOR HELSREACH!'

 

'They cannot hear you, brothers!'

 

'FOR HELSREACH!'

 

'Hurl yourselves at these walls, inhuman filth! Die on our blades! I am Grimaldus of the BLack Templars, and I will cast your carcasses from these holy walls!'

 

'GRIMALDUS! GRIMALDUS! GRIMALDUS!'


 
Grimaldus nodded, still staring out over the wastelands, letting the cheering chant mix with the howling wind, knowing it would carry to the advancing enemy.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
Just let's keep this short.

You find yourself as the commander of an imperial defence army and have to kill orks to save the hive world Armaggedon. Fair enough.

You do so by commanding a small army chosen from over 70 different units consisiting of infantry, tanks, vehicles planes and artillery per faction. Each unit has its own combination of weaponry, so basically no two units are the same. The turn based combat is sometimes unpredictable and scouting is hard, since each unit has only one move per turn.

There are some fairly easy missions, and then there are some crushingly hard ones, even those that you are not supposed to win, long time no seeing that!

All in all I love it! One of the best turn based strategy games for me since the battle isle games on my old DOS computer. 

Completed the main story (Imperial Steel Legion-Campaign) in about 30 hours.
So far I did not play the DLCs.

Too bad it lacks a multiplayer and has some strange missing file bugs during cutscenes, which will give you a headache.

review:
Panzer General 40.000: Armageddon, is a good tactical strategy game. I'm about 2/3rds through the main campaign, and enjoying it quite a bit. Those people complaining and obsessing about the turn limit, don't seem to get that this is an integral part of the challenge: you have to complete your objectives in time! THe turn limit is not just effective for the gameplay, but also realistic.

Obviously this is not an AAA title. Some of the voice acting is not so great, the units are not fully animated, etc. But I think it is all fine, for the genre. Not sure it's worth almost 37€ though. I would probably have price it at around 25€.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
Simply having every single forgeworld variant of a GW model a good game does not make. It's Final Liberation yet... not as good.  

Nothing new to bed said that isn't said in other reviews, so in summary:

-Hideously overpriced - I got it on sale for $20 and still felt a bit ripped off
-Rushing is pretty much the only viable tactic given the time constraint
-The enemy are legion, and you really get curb stomped a lot of the time as your raw recruits get hit by veteran Nobz en masse
-Having so many unit variants is great... unless you aren't incredibly familiar with GW 40K. I've been involved for over 20 years, so I'm pretty savvy on the differences between a Leman Russ Exterminator, Punisher, and Vindicator.... but for a "lay person" it's just overwhelming. Less is more guys! 
-The overall gameplay feels shallow and unispired, especially when you can get a SIGNIFICANTLY better game with Panzercorp, or the newer Order of Battle: World War (don't be a dupe, it's NOT free to play - it's a bit of a dickish way to give a demo you then pay to play various campaigns)

That is all. Glory to the Emperor.

An army that would normally cost thousands of pounds/dollars and hundreds of hours to amass, is created at the click of a button. All the Leman Russ Variants are in here. Same with most of the Imperial Super Heavy tanks. Also, yes, Titans. Warhounds and Reavers for days. 

Now, admittedly, the graphics and sound are very basic. Animations are non-existent. The turn limit is rather annoying and the difficulty varies from level to level. However, if you want to feel like you are commanding a huge Imperial force on the battlefield, this game is for you.
